key_insight: A unified planar 3D ILSA workflow, combined with matrix splitting and Anderson acceleration, can substantially cut hydraulic-fracture simulation time while keeping results close to the original solver.
engineering_relevance: These speedups make planar 3D hydraulic-fracture models more practical for optimization studies and mini-frac interpretation where many runs are needed.
method: Merged the elastohydrodynamic solver and front-tracking loops into one iterative scheme.
Split the linearized system so the dense elasticity part moves to the right-hand side, leaving a sparser matrix to solve.
Applied Anderson acceleration and tested it with a predictor–corrector scheme.
evidence_signal: Across five benchmark cases, the unified scheme averaged 2.5x speedup, the full combination averaged 4x, and the sandglass case reached 11x while keeping aperture error below 5%.
watch_item: The added acceleration and splitting steps may be geometry-dependent, so performance and stability should be revalidated for new fracture shapes and parameter regimes.
paper_title: Acceleration methods for the planar 3D ILSA hydraulic fracturing model
